name: Logitech Lift Vertical Ergonomic Mouse, Wireless

specs.Angle: 57° vertical angle — natural handshake position reduces forearm pronation
specs.Size: Compact — fits small-to-medium hands (under ~19cm hand length)
specs.Connectivity: Logi Bolt USB receiver OR Bluetooth 5.0
specs.Variants: Available in right-handed and left-handed versions, multiple colors

whatWorks: The 57° vertical angle addresses repetitive strain injury risk at the core — it keeps the forearm in a neutral handshake position rather than the flat pronated position of standard mice. For users with existing wrist or forearm discomfort, the switch to a vertical mouse often provides immediate relief.

The compact size targets users with small-to-medium hands who find the MX Vertical's large grip overwhelming. Most mainstream vertical mice assume large hands; the Lift's shorter height and tighter grip shape is a meaningful differentiator for the significant portion of users who find full-size ergonomic mice uncomfortable.

Dual connectivity — Logi Bolt USB receiver or Bluetooth 5.0 — covers both scenarios: a desktop without Bluetooth uses the receiver; a laptop with Bluetooth and limited USB ports uses wireless pairing without occupying a port.

Twenty-four months of battery life from a single AA battery makes the Lift essentially maintenance-free in practice. Most users won't change the battery more than once a year, and the low battery indicator gives advance warning before it dies.
worthKnowing: The Lift is designed for small-to-medium hands specifically. Users with hands larger than about 19cm may find the grip cramped, particularly with the pinky finger having limited support. The MX Vertical ($149) is the right choice for large-handed users who want a vertical mouse from Logitech.

The 4000 DPI maximum is adequate for standard office productivity at typical monitor resolutions, but falls short for high-DPI large-format monitors or users who want fine pixel-level control for design work. The Lift is a productivity mouse, not a precision tool.
The scroll wheel is a standard click-to-click wheel without the MagSpeed freewheel capability of the MX Master 3S. For navigating long documents, the Lift's scrolling is functional but not as fast.

The vertical design has an adjustment period — most users need 1-3 weeks before vertical mousing feels as natural as a standard mouse. Wrist position improvement is typically felt quickly; fine motor precision at the new angle takes longer to develop.

commonComplaints.title: Too small for large hands…
commonComplaints.body: My hand is just over 20cm and the Lift leaves my fingers hanging awkwardly off the front. Needs to be larger for big-handed users — the MX Vertical is the right fit for me.

commonComplaints.title: Takes a few weeks to feel natural…
commonComplaints.body: The first week felt awkward and my accuracy was noticeably off. By week three it felt completely natural. Stick with it through the adjustment period.

commonComplaints.title: Scroll wheel is basic…
commonComplaints.body: A notched click-to-click scroll wheel on a $89 mouse is fine, but coming from a MagSpeed wheel it feels slow for long-document navigation. Does the job.

commonComplaints.title: Only one Bluetooth device at a time…
commonComplaints.body: The Lift connects to one device at a time — no easy-switch between two computers. You have to re-pair to switch. The Logi Bolt receiver is more convenient if you use one main machine.

verdict: The Logitech Lift is the best-value entry into ergonomic vertical mice, particularly for users with small-to-medium hands who find most vertical mice too large. The 57° angle and proper grip shape provide the forearm comfort benefit without the size or price of the MX Vertical, and the 24-month battery life with dual connectivity make it a low-maintenance daily driver. The two-to-three-week adjustment period is the only real barrier, and the wrist comfort payoff makes the transition worth it for most users.

realBuyers.quote: My wrist pain from long hours of mouse use disappeared within two weeks of switching to the Lift. I was skeptical it would make a real difference. It did.
Perfect size for my medium hands — the MX Vertical was too tall and the grip was too wide. The Lift fits exactly right. This is the vertical mouse Logitech should have made first.
Battery life is absurd in the best way. Changed the battery once in 16 months of daily use. Barely a maintenance item.
The left-handed version availability is why I bought Logitech. Most ergonomic mice are right-hand only. The Lift being available in a true left-hand version is genuinely appreciated.

metaDescription: Logitech Lift Vertical Ergonomic Mouse review for Canada: 57° angle, 24-month battery, Bluetooth or receiver. Best vertical mouse for small hands. ~$89 CAD.
introText: The Logitech Lift Vertical Ergonomic Mouse (ASIN B09BNX5NCC) is a compact vertical mouse at 57° angle with 24-month AA battery life and Logi Bolt or Bluetooth 5.0 connectivity, available on Amazon.ca at around $89 CAD.
